chunks.note: Do this before anything else — the folder unlocks everything below

chunks.label: Study Session 1: Open your notes and list every major topic Hume covers — just make the list, nothing else
Study Session 2: Open your notes and list every major topic Kant covers — just make the list, nothing else
Study Session 3: Pick the 3 hardest Hume concepts from your list and explain each one out loud or in writing in your own words
chunks.note: Use your uploaded docs to check yourself after

chunks.label: Study Session 4: Pick the 3 hardest Kant concepts from your list and explain each one out loud or in writing in your own words
chunks.note: Use your uploaded docs to check yourself after

chunks.label: Study Session 5: Do a timed practice run — answer 3-5 likely exam questions from memory, then review gaps
chunks.note: Complete this at least 1 day before April 13th

chunks.label: Do Practice exams in Claude
Practice Exams again

Pick your 8 documents: Skim through Chapters 13–17 of Texas Voices and circle or list 8 primary source documents that each have 3 Thought Questions after them (skip any that only have 1 question after a picture).
Read Document 1 and answer all 3 of its Thought Questions in a few sentences each.
Read Documents 2 & 3 and answer all 3 Thought Questions for each.
Read Documents 4 & 5 and answer all 3 Thought Questions for each.
Read Documents 6 & 7 and answer all 3 Thought Questions for each.
Read Document 8 and answer all 3 of its Thought Questions, then do a quick pass to make sure every document has all 3 answers filled in.
Format and submit: Organize your answers clearly (label each document and question), do a final read-through, and turn it in.
Read Ch. 12 (all), pp. 299–319 – Progressive Era / Jim Ferguson
Read Ch. 13 (all), pp. 325–349 – The 1920s
Read Ch. 14, pp. 355–360 – Beginning of the Great Depression
Read Ch. 14, pp. 361–367 – Texas and the New Deal I
Read Ch. 14, pp. 367–379 – Texas and the New Deal II
Read Ch. 15 (all), pp. 385–405 – Texas and World War II
Read Ch. 16, pp. 411–435 – Texas in the 1950s
Read Ch. 17, pp. 441–449 – The 1960s I
Complete all reading sections (see Reading List project)
Go through list of key terms and scale each one based on understanding
Look up and review only the terms you blanked on or felt shaky about
Take your first mock quiz or practice test
Take a second mock quiz or practice test (different questions if possible)
Do a final mock test timed like the real exam
Make your one allowed note card to bring to class
Spend 5 min thinking about one thing your mom loves or has mentioned lately
Open Amazon (or one store) and search that one thing — set a 10 min timer so you don't spiral
Pick ONE gift under your budget and add it to cart — don't overthink it, she'll love it
Check shipping estimate to make sure it arrives by May 9
Complete the purchase
Go to a drugstore or dollar section and grab a card (or open Canva/a free e-card site if easier)
Write 2-3 sentences in the card — a memory, something you appreciate, and 'I love you'
Skim the Arendt text: read only the first and last paragraph of each section to get the big picture (20-30 min)
Read the Arendt text fully in one sitting — use a timer, 25 min on / 5 min off (Pomodoro)
Read the professor's companion chapter — highlight 3-5 key ideas that connect to the Arendt text
Write 5 questions you think could show up on the quiz based on your reading
Ask Claude to run a practice quiz on the Arendt text (10-15 questions)
Review any questions you missed and re-read those sections
Do one final practice quiz with Claude to confirm you're ready
Re-read the assignment instructions in the MODULE titled 'Writing Assignments & Presentations' and pick 1 person you admire — they don't have to be a politician or even alive.
Open the library database webguide (also in that MODULE) and find 3 academic sources about your chosen candidate.
Copy the 3 source links/titles somewhere safe (Notes app, doc, anywhere) so you don't lose them.
Write a rough draft of your 500-word essay explaining why you chose this person — don't worry about it being perfect yet.
Format your Word doc with your Name, Govt. section, Date, Candidate's name, and paste in your essay.
Use the webguide's citation resources to format your 3 sources in MLA or APA and add them to the bottom of the doc.
Do a final read-through and submit the Word doc before May 24.
Finish blocker assignments first (Candidate Essay + Candidate Platform) — these must be done before you can start the presentation
Open PowerPoint and create a title slide with your candidate's name, a photo, and the tagline '2028 Presidential Campaign'
Build slides 2–5: Who is your candidate? Include their background, major civil rights contributions, and 1–2 images of them or their cause
Build slides 6–9: Pick 3–4 current issues (e.g. immigration, gun control, healthcare) and add one slide per issue showing your candidate's stance in bullet points
Add a slide naming at least one interest group that would support your candidate and briefly explain why
Polish the deck — make slides colorful, remove walls of text, and add images to any slides that are bare
Add a Works Cited slide listing all sources and image credits (this slide does not count toward your 10-slide minimum)
Open the worksheet file (PresidentialCandidatePlatform-2.docx) and read through all the prompts so you know exactly what's being asked — don't fill anything in yet, just get familiar with it. (5 min)
Pick your fictional presidential candidate: choose a name, a political party (real or made-up), and jot down 2–3 issues they care most about. This is your foundation. (10–15 min)
Fill in the platform positions section of the worksheet — write 1–3 sentences per issue explaining where your candidate stands and why. Aim for at least 3 issues total. (20–30 min)
Complete any remaining worksheet sections (biography, slogan, goals, etc.) — use bullet points first if full sentences feel slow. You can polish later. (20–30 min)
Review your completed worksheet and highlight or star the 3–5 strongest platform points that should be front-and-center in your Campaign Presentation. (10 min)
Start building your Presidential Campaign Presentation using the worksheet as your outline — make sure every worksheet item is represented somewhere in your slides or speech. (30–45 min)
Do a final check: confirm the completed worksheet is attached/included with your submission alongside the presentation, as the instructions require. (5 min)

Gather everything: collect all notes, slides, past quizzes, and any study guides into one folder (physical or digital). Just collecting, nothing else.
Skim all materials for 20 min: flip through everything without reading deeply — just flag topics that feel unfamiliar or scary with a sticky note or highlight.
Review past quizzes: go through each quiz you've taken, note which questions you got wrong, and write a one-sentence explanation of the right answer.
Deep review — Part 1: pick the first half of the course topics and spend one focused session re-reading notes or slides on those only.
Deep review — Part 2: repeat the same focused session for the second half of the course topics.
Practice quiz with Claude: ask Claude to quiz you on the topics you flagged as fuzzy. Do it out loud or typed, low stakes, just to see what sticks.
Full practice exam with Claude: ask Claude to simulate a full cumulative exam under mild time pressure. Review every answer afterward.
Final weak-spot pass: based on the practice exam, spend one last session only on the 2–3 topics where you lost the most points.

debriefs.did: I took my final exam today and secured an A in the class. I did an insta cart run and spent entirely too much time building out our companion website for me and my dad's fantasy team. I also helped my parents set up their old laptop for my grandmother to use.
debriefs.enough: I finished my Maymester class with an A grade. I am killing it at school and now I get to just focus on insta cart and enjoying some free time before my summer class starts

debriefs.did: I turned in my 2 remaining assignments for my class, did another reading, did an insta cart run, and picked up my prescription and topped off on gas. It was a light day, but I earned a bit of a break after working so hard the last couple weeks
debriefs.enough: I earned a light day, and nothing important got put off. I enjoyed my free time with my friends and that is important for not burning myself out

debriefs.did: I completed my final government project, did another chapter quiz, and did some insta cart. I have done a fantastic job managing this class
debriefs.enough: I am way ahead of schedule for my Maymester class and am on track to get another A.

debriefs.did: I finished one of my government projects, I read my philosophy assignment, and I got a TON of stuff done for my DND campaign
debriefs.enough: Im getting things done on time. Im not perfect, but nothing is on fire, and I handle things when their due dates approach.

debriefs.did: Spent several hours doing a deep dive on power systems concepts — reactive power, impedance, transmission line wave behavior, characteristic impedance, and reflection. Reignited something that had been missing for weeks. Also identified that my Concerta may be causing a lot of my persistent negative feelings and wrote to my psychiatrist about it.
debriefs.fire: But the side effects of my medication are worth monitoring until I hear back from my psychiatrist. Tomorrow i will get back on track with my history readings
debriefs.enough: I reignited my passion for the field I'm building toward, identified something important about how my medication might be affecting me, and took action on it. The empty feeling was real, but it didn't stop me from doing something genuinely meaningful today.

debriefs.did: not much, im really tired of feeling so bad. Its killing my motivation
debriefs.fire: I didnt really do my philosophy reading. I'll cram for it tomorrow and do ok, but today was a bad day
debriefs.enough: It wasn't today was a bad day

debriefs.did: I did a lot today. I started my tax return process, I worked on my dashboard, I did my laundry, washed my sheets, cleaned up the house, vacuumed the rug and stairs, and worked through a tough day mentally. I also studied for my Hume/Kant exam
debriefs.enough: Today was tough, and I still got a lot done. Nothing is on fire, just keep working through and journaling and everything will work itself out
I did a ton of work today. I still have time to do everything I need to do. I will adjust and move forward, but I did well today, I worked really hard

debriefs.did: I completed every encounter and npc needed for the campaign tomorrow. The rest can easily be completed tomorrow
debriefs.enough: I got what i needed to done. Everything else can be managed

debriefs.did: I finished my index card for tomorrows exam, I did 2 additional study sessions, I further improved my dashboard, I made my bed, I sent my phsych my BP readings, and I contacted my doctor about my BP meds.
debriefs.enough: Today was a tough day emotionally because of my withdrawal from Bupropion, and i still managed to finish studying and handle a few life tasks that were important. Nothing import got pushed

debriefs.did: I did my laundry, did an insta cart run, did some reading towards my history exam, and scheduled my classes so they are ready for registration. I also read chapter 12 of my text book, so all i have left for history is to finish reading the final chapter and study. I have done well the last couple days
debriefs.enough: I have caught up to where i need to be for Texas History and did another Instacart run.

debriefs.did: I met with an advisor and planned out the next few semesters of school, I did a couple hours of insta cart, and I read most of capter 8 from my us history text book. I still have a couple tasks undone, but I am going to give myself grace on those, I did the 2 most important tasks for today, and I have been sleeping very poorly latetly, making it harder to be productive today.
debriefs.fire: Texas History is behind schedule, but it is manageable. I just need to spend a couple hours tomorrow to catch up and I'll be done
debriefs.enough: I did the 2 most important tasks, I did an insta cart run, and I still have plenty of time to complete my upcoming projects by their due dates. Not every day has to be perfect, it just needs to be enough, and today was enough

debriefs.did: Studied for philosophy before class. Chose a meeting and created an outline for the public meeting project. Read Chapter 7 for Texas History. Built the Personal OS dashboard as a tool to better organize and visualize proof of successful long-term task management.
debriefs.enough: I am doing enough, I am doing well, and this dashboard will help reinforce that until the defense mechanism starts to fade.
